title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee met on May 11, 2026, approving minutes and permits, awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding. The committee also directed staff to develop $100/day fines for illegal sewer discharges and approved construction service proposals for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
2025 Water Utility PSC annual report review

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project now likely moved to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repair work by Integrity Grading, Schofield Avenue punch list items with HOS Construction, and the retention pond modeling showing increased phosphorus removal benefit for Weston.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Spring activities included mailbox and sign repairs, curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, and storm damage cleanup from an East Nick area storm. Dewatering pumps were deployed for about a week near Weston Avenue tributary due to high water levels. Committee discussed need for permanent pump solutions for the Crane Meadows area flooding issues. A culvert failure on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration issues in the sanitary sewer system. Data showed April had 37 million gallons pumped into the distribution system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water from infiltration or illegal sump pump discharges. The Kathleen lift station showed one house's sump pump was causing nearly continuous pumping. INI study with in-pipe technologies is planned to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with 6.7% infrastructure placed in service including wells 7 and 8. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution requests the state develop long-term sustainable transportation funding solutions for local governments. Current state funding doesn't adequately support recurring costs like street maintenance and snow plowing, requiring general tax dollar subsidies. The resolution notes Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

Michael reported on quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. PJ was at $4.25/ton. Analysis showed the village's recycling program saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, totaling about $130,000 in savings on this year's three projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of ordinance prohibiting illegal sump pump discharges to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee member Mumper distinguished between unknowing versus willful violations. After discussion about appropriate penalty levels and noting the significant cost impact (approximately $30,000-$42,000 extra treatment costs in recent months), committee directed staff to develop enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael explained a duplex developer at 2230 Radkey Avenue needs temporary utility connection via long lateral until Radkey Avenue is reconstructed. Chair Zagami requested a specific date be added to the agreement. Motion passed to authorize staff to enter agreement with assessment for main extension when Radkey is reconstructed no later than end of 2029 construction season.

Committee reviewed agreement with Village of Rothschild for utility work on Military Road reconstruction. Weston's share is $94,860 for sanitary sewer ($6,156) and water main relocation ($88,704). Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend board approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services on two projects. For Alderson/Jelinek roundabout, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910 - MSA recommended due to lower cost and roundabout design involvement. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730 - Clark Dietz recommended despite slightly higher cost because MSA proposed using a summer intern while Clark Dietz would use full-time experienced staff. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: April 13, 2026 meeting minutes approved
April 2026 water and sewer permits acknowledged
2025 Water Utility PSC annual report acknowledged
Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding adopted - to be sent to governor and legislators
King Gravel awarded 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Staff directed to develop sanitary sewer discharge violation enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day
Staff authorized to enter utility connection agreement with 2230 Radkey Avenue property owner with assessment due when street reconstructed no later than 2029
Village Board to consider Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild ($94,860 Weston share)
MSA approved for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout construction services ($37,315)
Clark Dietz approved for Bladel Avenue construction services ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting scheduled for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday in eastbound lanes
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for next meeting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair with a second by Jackson and elected unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected Secretary unanimously. There was brief procedural confusion about whether the Vice Chair nomination had been seconded.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the event, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel room usage. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a motion carried unanimously. The commission asked for detailed hotel data in the post-event report.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, describing the venue and meeting the candidates. She noted winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees and the candidates were impressed with what Marathon County had to offer. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing technical details about gas pipeline easements and future building expansion.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained challenges with building orientation due to a gas pipeline, noting the building was rotated to face east to avoid the easement while utilizing the higher elevation of the site. The building is designed for 50-60 officer capacity for future growth, with expansion possible to the north. An alternate storage shed was discussed for evidence overflow. Motion to approve with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented the new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection for structural integrity and a winter operational inspection for material handling practices. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT's was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and will be contacted. Motion to approve with reporting to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion included potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has a presentation on qualifications they will share with the commission.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing past issues with the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure oversight doesn't get 'lost in the sauce' like some other zoning complaints.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted with twice-annual inspections in wellhead protection areas
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to contact existing salt storage facility near Sternberg about wellhead protection compliance
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF district qualifications with commission
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veterans Service Office, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. No major policy votes were taken; the meeting focused on educating committee members about departmental missions, budgets, and operations.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote attendance confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Overview of standing committee operations and agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, including missions, duties, and jurisdiction
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veterans Service Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.item: Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting aye.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinance and structure
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained how the Health & Human Services Committee operates under section 2.04 sub 5 of county ordinances, including its jurisdiction over the Health Department, Social Services, Veterans Service Office, and affiliated multi-county entities like North Central Health Care and the ADRC. He explained reporting relationships with statutory boards including the Social Services Board, Veterans Service Commission, Board of Health, and Transportation Coordinating Committee.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect against environmental hazards. She highlighted 1,200+ licenses managed by environmental health, communicable disease response capabilities including measles preparedness, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, a new homeless system response position, and public health vending machines offering free supplies including tick kits. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE across four teams.

Administrator Leonard covered the department's divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. With approximately 140 FTE, it is the county's second largest department. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing housing instability for child welfare and youth justice families, noting significant success helping families without prior banking, credit, or lease experience.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his office of 3.0 FTE had 8,149 customer interactions in 2025, up from approximately 5,900. He noted $44 million annually in VA benefits flowing to Marathon County veterans, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues and HUD-VASH, and suicide prevention efforts following 7 veteran suicides among 30 total county suicides in 2025. He is pursuing federal suicide prevention grants.

Executive Director Mike Ray described the four-county integrated agency serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with approximately 60 FTEs and 385-400 volunteers. Services include information and assistance, dementia care, falls prevention, Medicare/Medicaid navigation, and Meals on Wheels nutrition program. He noted significant financial challenges with federal Older Americans Act funding flat since 2024 while nutrition costs increased 40-45%, requiring service reductions. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135.

actionItems: Schedule North Central Healthcare for separate presentation before committee
Chair Boots to present strategic plan information at upcoming meetings
Next meeting confirmed as Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from originally stated May 29th)
Committee members to email agenda item suggestions to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinances amending mobile food vendor licensing, nuisances, and e-bike regulations, and engaged in extensive discussion about parking challenges at the Kennedy Park farmers market location. The board also made appointments to the CLIPS committee and approved various license renewals.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and immediately adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Unfinished business - remote meeting attendance policy amendments
New business - committee appointments and secondhand dealer license
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
discussions.body: Staff presented a proposal to increase park impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 to $563 for duplex units, and to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke in public comment questioning whether the village has real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Barb spoke in favor, stating the fee is justified as population growth increases park needs. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was also approved unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 of the elected officials handbook regarding remote meeting attendance. Trustees discussed removing language requiring specific health, safety, or welfare justifications, instead making the policy broader. An amendment by Trustee Beck to strike sentences requiring justification explanations passed with Trustees Herling and Lopesorrell voting no. The original motion as amended then passed with the same two dissenting votes.

Chief Finkey explained this creates a regional inspection sticker system where fire inspections from one participating municipality (Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and SAFER communities) are recognized by all others, eliminating redundant inspections for food trucks. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Farmers market location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Discussion-only item addressing concerns about the market's new location after losing the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue site to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about customers parking on grass along Alderson Street and in the park. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the old municipal building to 42 at Camp Phillips to now 53 at Kennedy Park, with vendors now able to keep vehicles with them. Some trustees suggested discontinuing the market if suitable location cannot be found. Renee clarified the market is not losing money and operates using grants for food assistance programs.

discussions.item: Secondhand article and jewelry dealer license
discussions.body: The board approved a secondhand article dealer and secondhand jewelry dealer license for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief explained this involves buying and selling precious metals with large dollar transactions. The motion was amended to require satisfactory completion of background check and receipt of occupancy permit, as the location is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Golden Mua may be considered for Finance committee after settling into the first committee. Motion approved unanimously. Openings remain for Parks, two for HR/Finance, and one more for CLIPS.

During public works report, Michael Dolan reported on the ongoing speed limit issue from two board meetings ago. The DOT engineering study showed it should be a 35 mph road, but traffic data showed 85th percentile speed was 45 mph. Another speed study is needed after construction ends. A resident had inquired as the speed is currently inconsistent with the village ordinance.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a SAFER board member before official appointment, and complained about lack of detailed Kennedy Park information in the packet. He also spoke during the park impact fee public hearing questioning whether the village has actual data on park maintenance costs to justify the fee increase.
actionItems: Ordinance 26-010 approved increasing park and recreation impact fees for new residential development
Resolution 2026-015 approved amending the village fee schedule for park impact fees
Ordinance 26-008 approved creating regional mobile food vendor inspection reciprocity
Ordinance 26-009 approved amending standing committees section
Ordinance 26-011 approved amending chapter 50 nuisances
Ordinance 26-012 approved amending bicycle/e-bike regulations in chapter 82
Resolution 2026-014 adopted supporting long-term sustainable funding for state transportation fund
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC pending background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan (CAP) approved
All consent agenda license renewals approved
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspection assistance
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 pm
Staff to bring back speed limit study for Weston Avenue after construction ends
Committee/commission applications to be included in board packets going forward

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions, and unanimously passed a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris following the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board heard from affected residents requesting additional fee relief beyond the proposed reduction.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call (technical difficulties with meeting manager)
Committee appointments remarks by Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks on committee appointment philosophy, emphasizing diversity of perspectives, leadership development, and noting 34% of the board is in first or second terms. Vice chairs were assigned responsibility for shepherding the strategic plan. Supervisor Cavelli moved and Supervisor Crims seconded to approve all appointments. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for children and elderly residents. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Supervisor Cavelli moved and Supervisor Drebeck seconded. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for storm debris from the recent tornado and flooding. The state waived $13.10, Town of Ringle waived $2.33, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94/ton (commercial rate minus waivers) to cover operational costs. Leonard noted the county won't meet the $670,743 damage threshold for state disaster aid, but expects Ringle will. Supervisor Rosenberg requested public comment, and residents Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck spoke, asking the county to fully waive fees citing hardship and slow insurance responses. Multiple supervisors asked clarifying questions about fee structure, timeline, and future policy. The board unanimously suspended rules to consider the resolution, then passed it unanimously. Supervisor Arstead moved, Supervisor Morash seconded.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty asked Marathon County to fully waive its portion of tipping fees, noting that the state, DNR, and Ringle have waived their fees. She described community self-reliance efforts, with one business incurring $40,000 in cleanup costs, and requested a six-month fee waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of affected homeowners, noting that 44 homes need demolition, many insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived, and residents need extended relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved: Landfill tipping fees reduced to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term disaster debris fee policy within six months
Resolution 24-26 approved: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved: Social services budget amendment

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee met on May 5, 2026, approving two rezoning petitions unanimously and voting to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado, rather than the broader fee waiver originally proposed by staff.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Introduction to departments appearing before committee
Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
Conservation, Planning and Zoning Department presentation

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the rezoning from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property. Pogle explained the property has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. He confirmed no natural areas would be lost. Town of McMillan recommended approval April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve; Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. Supervisor Drew questioned the precedent this sets for future disasters. Supervisor Leur asked about potential levy impacts. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to property owners affected by smaller disasters. After debate, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution. Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then amended the motion to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, excluding other zoning and septic fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment passed with some opposition. The amended motion to forward to county board also passed with some opposition.

Dave Hagen Busher presented on Solid Waste operations, covering the landfill's history, tipping fees, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, and upcoming leachate treatment requirements by 2032. Tom Lovelin presented on Parks, Recreation and Forestry, noting the department serves both county and city of Wausau, operates 17 county parks and 30,700 acres of county forest. A Nine Mile study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million for the local economy. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - waiving only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems on tornado-damaged properties for one year
Two rezoning petitions forwarded to Marathon County Board of Supervisors for final approval
Conservation services director Kirsty Heiden to present at next meeting
Special summer educational meeting on conservation topics to be scheduled

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center to next month due to the chair's absence, approved signage for the Plover River Water Trail kayak launches, and received updates on Kennedy Park development and Yellow Banks disc golf course maintenance. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and questioned Kennedy Park fundraising transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Kennedy Park update and timeline presentation
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded with no discussion. The motion carried unanimously with all present voting in favor.

Director Sean Oster presented a timeline document tracing Kennedy Park development from the comprehensive outdoor recreation plan approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked about fundraising timelines, learning there is no fixed deadline but a 10-year goal for master plan completion. Staff clarified that village capital funds were removed from the capital improvement plan pending fundraising progress, and that Friends of Kennedy Park plans to announce fundraising totals at their June tournament. The committee discussed that the Friends group is currently focused on raising $117,000 for upper field lights, with approximately $85,000 already at the community foundation.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and information on tree removal from the river and installation of wood mats over gas pipelines for equipment access. Dan showed the committee the approximate locations where trees were removed and explained the mat installation process over TC Energy pipelines. The mats now belong to the village with extras available for future trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to next month because committee chair Katrina, who had requested the discussion, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy (6504 [address]) spoke during public comment opposing dogs at the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' The acting chair noted Andy's objection would be recorded.

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches on the trail, noting their sandblaster pressure washer cannot access the area due to bridge weight limits and terrain. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, and organizing volunteer work days. Committee members suggested community volunteers could help with maintenance given the trail's popularity. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate solutions.

Dan Houdek from Marathon County (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version seven of signage for the Plover River Water Trail. Signs will be installed at launches 3-8, with three in Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge levels, and location-specific history. Dan noted the Highway J launch will likely be closed all season due to bridge replacement. A motion to approve sign installation passed unanimously.

Staff raised a request received about installing food allergy awareness signage on playgrounds, noting signs would be provided at no cost. The committee agreed to bring this back for further discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process is beginning with a kickoff meeting with Rettler planned for the following Tuesday.

publicComment: Two public commenters spoke. Andy (6504 [street address inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Street, Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park fundraising transparency, calling the $8,700 funding report figures 'convoluted and misleading' and asking where money from the glow event and lighting fundraiser was being held.
actionItems: Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June 22, 2026 meeting
Approved installation of Plover River Water Trail kayak launch signs at three Weston locations (passed unanimously)
Staff to investigate graffiti removal and prevention options for Oaklair River Trail
Staff to bring food allergy awareness playground signage proposal to future meeting
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for following Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved minutes and acknowledged budget reports before receiving an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on clerk operations including elections, licensing, tax collection, and the newsletter. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and operations
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

A committee member asked about final numbers for TIF closure. Jessica explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, and the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. She confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt. The bonds are due October 1st.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented extensively on clerk operations. The office has three staff members (Pam and Sarah full-time, Leah part-time) down from four full-time previously. She covered elections (noting 84% of tax collection is done at the clerk's office counter, totaling $22 million), licensing (51 liquor licenses, numerous bartender licenses), pet licensing (brought back in-house in 2025 after 12 years with Pet Data), and the quarterly newsletter ($30,000 annual cost for printing and postage). She noted chief election inspector wages may need to increase from $13 to $15/hour to match county averages. Discussion occurred about the pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20, which was driven by Marathon County raising fees to cover their Humane Society costs.

Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well with expanded vendors including food trucks, coffee vendors, and crafts. Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving out of Weston. Concerns were raised about parking congestion near the farmers market on Alderson and Newport streets. The chair noted the committee now has open spots and encouraged recruitment of new citizen members.

actionItems: Minutes from previous meeting approved
April budget status report, TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Clerk to consider increasing chief election inspector wages to $15/hour for next election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th and took action on several items including recommending approval of nuisance ordinance amendments, an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on e-motos, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for a deteriorated structure. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations but deferred action pending staff research.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Written communications and department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Mobile food vendors fire inspection ordinance

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke critically about the new farmers market location, expressing concern that parking vehicles on grass violates village code and will destroy the park. He questioned ADA compliance for handicap parking on grass rather than asphalt, asked why fire inspections were being added for mobile food vendors, and criticized the age of some code enforcement cases that have been pending for over a year without visible progress.

Code Enforcement reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for all three municipalities. Reports were acknowledged by motion from Chwanka, seconded by Olsen, passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet switched) with language added for compliance with other jurisdictions' authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees. Passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred about regulating e-bikes, e-motos, and electric scooters. Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting e-motos from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee discussed whether to exempt children's play scooters but decided to rely on officer discretion. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka to recommend approval with amendment prohibiting e-motos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4 AM to 4 PM) is far outside the norm compared to surrounding municipalities which typically have 4-5 hour overnight windows. DPW Director Michael Wodalski suggested either eliminating the restriction or signing specific problem areas (6-8 locations). Chief Hunt preferred maintaining overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. Committee deferred action to July, directing staff to identify specific exception areas.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot where parishioners park on Sundays. Staff estimated cost at $2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk or $25,000-30,000 for lighted system. Committee was reluctant to set precedent of paying for requested crosswalks. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka to recommend approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs. Passed unanimously.

Fire Chief presented a regional mutual inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and SAFER. Any participating department can inspect food trucks, which receive a sticker valid across all jurisdictions. Chief noted one recent inspection failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Wausau opted out to maintain their own program. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey to approve Ordinance 26-8. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue for a new duplex. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski as having addressing issues. Motion approved unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to proceed with raze order for a structure abandoned since 2010-2011 that has been posted since 2021. The building was too deteriorated for fire department training burn. Current owners, who are current on taxes and running a small farm operation, had housed chickens in the structure. Owners prefer the village proceed with demolition, with costs added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment criticizing the new farmers market location at the park, expressing concerns about vehicles parking on grass violating village code, ADA compliance issues with handicap parking on grass, questioning the need for fire inspections on food trucks, and criticizing the lack of progress on long-standing code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance to Board with prohibition on e-motos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
DPW Director to identify 6-8 specific areas for potential parking restriction signage and bring back in July
DPW Director to contact Mount Olive Church about paying for crosswalk installation on Alderson Street
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements
Approve address corrections for Delicoski Street, Ross Avenue, and Radkey Avenue properties
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
